items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: Why was Vesalius significant in the history of medicine?
items.correct: His 1543 De Humani Corporis Fabrica corrected Galen's errors via human dissection and accurate illustration, founding modern anatomy though not curing disease.
items.misconception: Vesalius discovered the cause of disease and ended humoral theory.
items.why: Vesalius improved anatomy but disease theory still ran on humours for centuries. Students conflate progress in one area (structure) with progress in another (cause of illness).

items.q: Why was the founding of the NHS controversial in 1948?
items.correct: The British Medical Association opposed nationalisation of doctors; Bevan made concessions allowing private practice; Conservatives feared cost and central control.
items.misconception: The NHS was supported by all parties and all doctors from the start.
items.random_wrong: The NHS was originally only for soldiers returning from WWII.
items.why: Bevan said he had to 'stuff their mouths with gold' to get doctors on board. Students project today's cross-party support backwards onto a deeply contested founding.

items.q: In what year was the Domesday Book completed?
items.why: It's 1086. “1066” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What was the name of William's brutal northern campaign?
items.why: Correct answer: Harrying of the North. “The Anarchy” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was the Battle of Hastings fought?
items.why: Exact answer: 1066 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
